title: Welsh Rarebit
subtitle: A thick cheese sauce of cheddar, stout, and mustard, grilled golden on toast
excerpt: Welsh rarebit is not cheese on toast. It is a cooked sauce (a roux enriched with cheddar, stout, mustard, and Worcestershire sauce) spread thickly on toast and grilled until deeply golden and bubbling. The sauce can be made ahead and the whole dish assembled in under 10 minutes.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: Welsh rarebit entry. The dish is documented from the early 18th century in British cookbooks, appearing as 'Welsh rabbit' in Hannah Glasse's The Art of Cookery (1747).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce can be made up to 3 days ahead and refrigerated. Reheat gently before spreading on toast.
recipe.temperatureNote: Made on the hob and finished under a hot grill; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of fat and flour cooked together briefly before liquid is added. The starch in the flour swells as it heats and absorbs the liquid, thickening it into a sauce. Equal parts butter and flour by weight is the standard ratio for a coating-consistency sauce.

content.text: Welsh rarebit is a cooked cheese sauce applied to toast and finished under the grill. The sauce is built on a roux (butter and flour cooked out before the liquid goes in) which gives it a body and stability that melted cheese alone cannot provide. It spreads thickly, holds its shape under the grill, and browns to a deep amber without running off the bread.
Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 1 minute to form a

. Gradually whisk in the {{stout}} of stout and {{whole-milk}} of milk, a little at a time, until smooth. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 2 minutes, stirring constantly, until the sauce is thick and smooth.

Remove from the heat. Stir in the {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar, the mustard powder, and the Worcestershire sauce. Beat in the egg yolk. Season with black pepper. The sauce should be thick enough to hold its shape when spread.
Heat the grill to its highest setting. Toast the {{bread-white}} bread slices until pale golden on both sides. Lay on a baking tray.

Spoon the sauce generously over each slice, spreading it right to the edges. Place under the hot grill for 3 to 4 minutes until deeply golden and bubbling. Watch carefully; it can burn quickly under a high grill.

items.cause: Cheese added to a sauce that was too hot, causing the proteins to tighten and squeeze out the fat.
items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat before stirring in the cheese. The residual warmth is enough to melt it without causing separation.

items.cause: Too thin, or spread while the toast was still too hot.
items.fix: The sauce must be thick enough to sit in a mound when dropped from a spoon. If it is too thin, return to the heat and stir until it thickens further. Let the toast cool slightly before spreading.

items.symptom: Topping is pale and does not brown
items.cause: Grill not hot enough or placed too far from the element.
items.fix: The grill must be at its highest setting and the tray placed as close to the element as the oven allows.

content.text: Welsh rarebit is documented from at least the early 18th century, appearing in Hannah Glasse's The Art of Cookery as 'Welsh rabbit', a name that has puzzled food historians ever since. The most persuasive explanation is that it was a satirical name for a Welsh dish that offered cheese as a substitute for meat. By the Victorian period it had been standardised as a cooked sauce dish, and the Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton's version is close to what most recipes still produce today. The stout version is a 20th-century elaboration; earlier recipes used ale or wine.
